question_title: Will the interest in “jarvis butts” change between 2026-03-30 and 2026-04-11 according to Google Trends?
summary: Jarvis Butts (convicted killer of Na'Ziyah Harris) died in prison on Mar 26, 2026, causing a Google Trends spike to 100. The value has rapidly decayed to 4 by Mar 30 and will almost certainly return to baseline (0) well before Apr 11. With start=4, "Decreases" requires end=0 exactly (since 4-0=4 > 3), while "Doesn't change" covers end=1-7. The main uncertainty is (1) whether SerpAPI reports the start value as 3 instead of 4 (making Decreases impossible), and (2) whether random noise pushes Apr 11 from 0 to 1. Historical data shows this obscure term sits at 0 for extended periods when not in the news. Decreases is the most likely outcome, but DC retains substantial probability due to measurement uncertainty at the floor.

factors.description: Post-spike decay pattern: value went 100→30→10→5→4 in 4 days. Baseline for this obscure term is 0. By Apr 11 (12 days out), extremely likely to be at 0.
Threshold arithmetic at start=4: Decreases requires end=0 exactly. DC covers end=1-7 (7 integer values). The asymmetric possibility space slightly favors DC over Decreases at any given moment.
SerpAPI measurement uncertainty (±1-2 vs pytrends). If SerpAPI reports start=3 instead of 4, Decreases is literally impossible (would need end < 0). ~10-15% chance of this.
Historical baseline is 0 for extended periods: 23 consecutive zeros (Feb 17 - Mar 11), 6 consecutive zeros (Mar 20-25). When no news, this term registers 0.
3-month base rate: no_change 92.2%, but this includes mostly 0→0 transitions. Not representative of post-spike decay. Trailing (6-day) rates: Decreases 50%, DC 33%.
Potential catalysts: Michigan State Police investigation into Butts' death, Na'Ziyah Harris remains search, media follow-ups. Low probability (~10-12%) of significant development near Apr 11.
At very low Google Trends values (0-5), noise and rounding mean even 1-2 random searches could register as 1 instead of 0, keeping the value within the DC band.

reasoning:
**Forecast: Decreases 58% / Doesn't change 36% / Increases 6%**

Key reasoning:

- **Context**: Jarvis Butts was convicted of murdering 13-year-old Na'Ziyah Harris and died by suicide in prison on Mar 26, causing a massive Google Trends spike (100). Interest has decayed rapidly to 4 by Mar 30.

- **Decreases (58%)**: The dominant scenario. Historical pattern shows this obscure term returns to 0 within ~8 days of any spike. By Apr 11 (12 days out), the value will almost certainly be 0. With start=4 and end=0, the drop of 4 exceeds the ±3 threshold.

- **Doesn't change (36%)**: Meaningful probability due to two edge cases: (1) SerpAPI might report start=3 (making Decreases literally impossible since you can't go below 0), and (2) random noise could register Apr 11 as 1 instead of 0 (keeping the change within ±3 of start=4).

- **Increases (6%)**: Requires a significant news catalyst (body found, investigation results) landing on or very near Apr 11. The ongoing Michigan State Police investigation and Na'Ziyah Harris remains search provide plausible but low-probability pathways.
resolution_criteria: This question resolves “Doesn't change” if the value on the timeline at [https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=jarvis%20butts&date=2026-03-12%202026-04-11](https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=jarvis%20butts&date=2026-03-12%202026-04-11) for 2026-04-11 is within 3 of the value at 2026-03-30. It resolves “Increases” if the value is more than 3 greater, and resolves “Decreases” if the value is more than 3 lower.
fine_print:
A script will be used to determine the resolution of this question. It will access the data on Google Trends using [SerpApi](https://serpapi.com/), and compare the height of the timeline for the two aforementioned dates. The specific python query will be structured as follows:
```
from serpapi import GoogleSearch

search = GoogleSearch(params={
  "api_key": API_KEY,
  "engine": "google_trends",
  "data_type": "TIMESERIES",
  "geo": "US",
  "tz": 0,
  "q": "jarvis butts",
  "date": "2026-03-12 2026-04-11",
})
search.get_dict()
```
Note that there may be differences between the results returned by the API and the data appearing on the page. This seems to be due to the 'tz' parameter not having the intended effect in-browser. In this case, the API results will be considered authoritative.
Note that the precision of the timeline will be 1 day, so this will compare the overall interest for the whole day as determined by Google Trends.
Dates are determined in UTC.
If the data is no longer available, or the script fails, this question will be annulled or manually resolved by a moderator.
